author: Robert Greene
positioning: Corporate survival tactics, decoded from the 3 books that actually teach workplace power.

items.title: Introduction
items.headline: The 48 Laws of Power
items.author: Robert Greene
items.series_label: THE 48 LAWS  ·  SUBSCRIBE FOR THE REST
items.tease: Office politics is a game — here's the playbook 🎯
items.comment_q: Office politics: play it or pretend it doesn't exist? 👇
items.body: Every workplace runs on power, not the org chart. Learn the moves or get moved.

items.title: Introduction
items.headline: The 48 Laws of Power
items.author: Robert Greene
items.series_label: THE 48 LAWS  ·  SUBSCRIBE FOR THE REST
items.tease: Pretending you're 'above politics' is why you're stuck ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is 'I just do good work' career advice or career suicide? 👇
items.body: The people who say politics is beneath them are the ones it happens to.

items.title: Introduction
items.headline: The 48 Laws of Power
items.author: Robert Greene
items.series_label: THE 48 LAWS  ·  SUBSCRIBE FOR THE REST
items.tease: Two equal performers. One gets promoted. Here's why 🎯
items.comment_q: Same output, different outcome — luck or strategy? 👇
items.body: Same results, same hours. One read the room, one didn't. Guess who's the manager now.

items.title: Law 1
items.headline: Never Outshine the Master
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Make your boss look brilliant in every meeting 🤝
items.comment_q: Outshine your manager publicly: bold move or career-ender? 👇
items.body: Sharpen your boss's idea, don't replace it. Hand them the spotlight; they hand you the promotion.

items.title: Law 1
items.headline: Never Outshine the Master
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Correcting your boss in public is quiet career suicide ⚠️
items.comment_q: Would you fix your boss's mistake in front of the team? 👇
items.body: You won the argument and lost the relationship. Being right in public costs you the next opening.

items.title: Law 1
items.headline: Never Outshine the Master
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Your idea, your boss's meeting — who gets the credit? 🎯
items.comment_q: Boss presents your work as theirs — speak up or bank it? 👇
items.body: Let them present it as theirs once. The chip you bank cashes bigger than the callout ever would.

items.title: Law 2
items.headline: Never Put Too Much Trust in Friends; Learn How to Use Enemies
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Your loudest critic is your most useful ally ⚔️
items.comment_q: Hire the person who challenged you publicly — yes or no? 👇
items.body: The coworker who fought you has something to prove. Put them on your project; they'll outwork your friends.

items.title: Law 2
items.headline: Never Put Too Much Trust in Friends; Learn How to Use Enemies
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Trusting your work-friend with everything will burn you ⚠️
items.comment_q: Real friends at work — asset or liability? 👇
items.body: The friend who knows your salary, your plans, your doubts is the leak you never see coming.

items.title: Law 2
items.headline: Never Put Too Much Trust in Friends; Learn How to Use Enemies
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You vented to a work-friend. It reached your boss 🎯
items.comment_q: Vent about your manager to a coworker: ever safe? 👇
items.body: One honest lunch, one repeated line, one cooled relationship with your boss. The friend felt 'safe.'

items.title: Law 3
items.headline: Conceal Your Intentions
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Job-hunt in silence, leave from strength 🤐
items.comment_q: Tell your manager you're interviewing — honest or naive? 👇
items.body: Don't announce you're looking. Build quietly, line up the offer, then negotiate from leverage, not hope.

items.title: Law 3
items.headline: Conceal Your Intentions
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Announcing your plans hands rivals the counter-move ⚠️
items.comment_q: Share your roadmap early or guard it till it's real? 👇
items.body: You floated the idea in standup. By the time you built it, someone with more clout 'owned' it.

items.title: Law 3
items.headline: Conceal Your Intentions
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: They asked your five-year plan. The safe answer 🎯
items.comment_q: Skip-level asks your ambitions — full honesty or filter? 👇
items.body: 'Where do you see yourself?' Reveal too much and you're a threat. Reveal too little and you're coasting. Aim narrow.

items.title: Law 4
items.headline: Always Say Less Than Necessary
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Stop over-explaining in meetings — it reads as weakness 🤐
items.comment_q: Loud in meetings or silent operator — who gets promoted? 👇
items.body: Say less than you want to. The person who explains the most in the room controls it the least.

items.title: Law 4
items.headline: Always Say Less Than Necessary
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: The 'just to clarify…' habit is killing your authority ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does over-clarifying make you look careful or junior? 👇
items.body: Every hedging preamble shrinks you. 'Just to clarify, I might be wrong, but—' tells them not to weigh it.

items.title: Law 4
items.headline: Always Say Less Than Necessary
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Your boss asks a loaded question. The 3-word answer that wins 🎯
items.comment_q: Loaded question from leadership — full answer or tight one? 👇
items.body: They asked to watch you scramble. You gave one clean sentence and stopped. Silence put the pressure back on them.

items.title: Law 5
items.headline: So Much Depends on Reputation — Guard It With Your Life
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Your reputation arrives before you do — engineer it 💼
items.comment_q: Reputation at work: built on results or visibility? 👇
items.body: Decide the two words you want said when you leave the room, then make every interaction prove them.

items.title: Law 5
items.headline: So Much Depends on Reputation — Guard It With Your Life
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: One missed deadline you didn't flag rewrites your whole rep ⚠️
items.comment_q: One slip-up — does it erase a year of good work? 👇
items.body: Reputation isn't your average; it's your worst unmanaged moment. Silence on a slip is what sticks.

items.title: Law 5
items.headline: So Much Depends on Reputation — Guard It With Your Life
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: New manager asks around about you. What they hear decides everything 🎯
items.comment_q: Should your rep precede you, or earn it fresh each time? 👇
items.body: Before the new director met you, three people described you in a sentence. That sentence set your ceiling.

items.title: Law 6
items.headline: Court Attention at All Costs
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Be impossible to ignore in the rooms that decide 📣
items.comment_q: Visible self-promotion: necessary or cringe? 👇
items.body: Quiet competence gets managed; visible competence gets promoted. Put your work where decision-makers actually look.

items.title: Law 6
items.headline: Court Attention at All Costs
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Doing great work nobody sees is a losing strategy ⚠️
items.comment_q: 'My work speaks for itself' — true or career myth? 👇
items.body: Work doesn't speak. People speak about work they noticed. Unseen excellence is just unpaid effort.

items.title: Law 6
items.headline: Court Attention at All Costs
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Two analysts, same output. One sends the Friday recap 🎯
items.comment_q: Weekly self-update to leadership: smart or try-hard? 👇
items.body: She sent a three-line Friday summary up the chain. He didn't. Leadership only knew one of them existed.

items.title: Law 7
items.headline: Get Others to Do the Work for You, but Always Take the Credit
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Leave at 6 and still get the credit 🏆
items.comment_q: Delegate the grunt work or grind it yourself for cred? 👇
items.body: Own the strategy and the visible result. Hand off the boring execution. Your name goes on the outcome.

items.title: Law 7
items.headline: Get Others to Do the Work for You, but Always Take the Credit
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Doing it all yourself is how you stay invisible ⚠️
items.comment_q: Hero-mode solo work: dedication or self-sabotage? 👇
items.body: You did everything so no one could fault you. No one could credit you either. Martyrs don't get promoted.

items.title: Law 7
items.headline: Get Others to Do the Work for You, but Always Take the Credit
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You did the work. Your teammate presented it. Now what 🎯
items.comment_q: Someone took credit for your work — confront or log it? 👇
items.body: He presented your analysis as his. You didn't explode — you cc'd the proof to the right inbox, calmly.

items.title: Law 8
items.headline: Make Other People Come to You — Use Bait if Necessary
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Make them come to you — control the terms 🪝
items.comment_q: Chase the opportunity or make it chase you? 👇
items.body: Don't beg for the project. Make your work the obvious answer so they bring it to you on your terms.

items.title: Law 8
items.headline: Make Other People Come to You — Use Bait if Necessary
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Always being available makes you the default, not the prize ⚠️
items.comment_q: Instant replies: reliable or easily taken for granted? 👇
items.body: Reply to everything in seconds and you become the help desk, not the hire they fight to keep.

items.title: Law 8
items.headline: Make Other People Come to You — Use Bait if Necessary
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You stopped volunteering for everything. They noticed 🎯
items.comment_q: Stop raising your hand for scraps — power move or risk? 👇
items.body: You quit grabbing every thankless task. Within a month they were asking you to take the ones that mattered.

items.title: Law 9
items.headline: Win Through Your Actions, Never Through Argument
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Win with the result, not the argument 🎯
items.comment_q: Debate it in the meeting or just ship the proof? 👇
items.body: Don't out-argue them. Ship the working version and let the outcome end the debate for you.

items.title: Law 9
items.headline: Win Through Your Actions, Never Through Argument
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Winning the meeting argument can lose you the room ⚠️
items.comment_q: Be right loudly or be right quietly — which wins long-term? 👇
items.body: You dismantled their logic in front of everyone. They remembered the humiliation, not your point.

items.title: Law 9
items.headline: Win Through Your Actions, Never Through Argument
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: They doubted your approach. You shipped it anyway 🎯
items.comment_q: Prove skeptics wrong with words or with a demo? 👇
items.body: Instead of defending the plan for a week, you built the prototype over a weekend. The demo ended the argument.

items.title: Law 10
items.headline: Infection: Avoid the Unhappy and Unlucky
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Distance yourself from the team's chronic complainer 🚪
items.comment_q: Is the office cynic dragging your reputation down too? 👇
items.body: Negativity is contagious and it's attributed by association. Be warm, be brief, be elsewhere.

items.title: Law 10
items.headline: Infection: Avoid the Unhappy and Unlucky
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Befriending the office martyr ties you to their ceiling ⚠️
items.comment_q: Guilty by association with the team pessimist — real or not? 👇
items.body: You're kind to the person everyone avoids. Leadership stopped separating their reputation from yours.

items.title: Law 10
items.headline: Infection: Avoid the Unhappy and Unlucky
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: The team's 'victim' wants you as their venting partner 🎯
items.comment_q: Comfort the chronic complainer or protect your energy? 👇
items.body: Every coffee became their grievance hour. Your output dipped, your mood dipped, and people noticed the pairing.

items.title: Law 11
items.headline: Learn to Keep People Dependent on You
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Become the one person the project can't run without 🔑
items.comment_q: Be indispensable or be easily replaced — your call? 👇
items.body: Own a piece of the work only you understand. Security isn't loyalty; it's being load-bearing.

items.title: Law 11
items.headline: Learn to Keep People Dependent on You
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Documenting yourself out of a job is a real risk ⚠️
items.comment_q: Share all your knowledge freely or keep an edge? 👇
items.body: You handed over every process doc, then wondered why you were the 'redundant' one in the reorg.

items.title: Law 11
items.headline: Learn to Keep People Dependent on You
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Layoffs hit. The team kept the person who knew the system 🎯
items.comment_q: In a cut, does irreplaceable beat well-liked? 👇
items.body: Two roles, one budget. They kept the one whose departure would break three things by Monday.

items.title: Law 12
items.headline: Use Selective Honesty and Generosity to Disarm Your Victim
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Disarm a rival with one unexpected, genuine assist 🤝
items.comment_q: Help a competitor at work: strategy or weakness? 👇
items.body: A single sincere favor to the right person buys more trust than a year of polite distance.

items.title: Law 12
items.headline: Use Selective Honesty and Generosity to Disarm Your Victim
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Pure self-interest with no goodwill leaves you exposed ⚠️
items.comment_q: All-business no-favors at work — efficient or isolating? 👇
items.body: You never helped anyone 'off the clock.' When you needed cover, the room was empty.

items.title: Law 12
items.headline: Use Selective Honesty and Generosity to Disarm Your Victim
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You helped the rival team hit their deadline. It paid back 🎯
items.comment_q: Bail out a rival team — naive or long game? 👇
items.body: You lent them your best person for two days. Next quarter their lead championed your promotion.

items.title: Law 13
items.headline: When Asking for Help, Appeal to Self-Interest, Never to Mercy or Gratitude
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Asking for help? Lead with what's in it for them 🎯
items.comment_q: Appeal to a colleague's interest or their kindness? 👇
items.body: Don't ask for mercy. Frame the ask as their win and the yes becomes easy.

items.title: Law 13
items.headline: When Asking for Help, Appeal to Self-Interest, Never to Mercy or Gratitude
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: 'As a favor to me' is the weakest ask at work ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does guilt-based asking ever actually work in an office? 👇
items.body: You reminded them how much you'd done for them. They heard a debt, not a reason, and stalled.

items.title: Law 13
items.headline: When Asking for Help, Appeal to Self-Interest, Never to Mercy or Gratitude
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You needed their team's help. You framed it as their metric 🎯
items.comment_q: Pitch a cross-team ask as your need or their gain? 👇
items.body: Instead of 'please, I'm drowning,' you showed how it hit their numbers too. Approved same day.

items.title: Law 14
items.headline: Pose as a Friend, Work as a Spy
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Learn what your stakeholders actually want — quietly 🕵️
items.comment_q: Read the room covertly or ask directly every time? 👇
items.body: In every 1:1 and hallway chat, gather what people really care about. Information is positioning.

items.title: Law 14
items.headline: Pose as a Friend, Work as a Spy
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Oversharing your read of people gets back to them ⚠️
items.comment_q: Tell a coworker you've 'figured them out' — bold or dumb? 👇
items.body: You bragged that you could read the VP. It traveled. Now the VP reads you as a threat.

items.title: Law 14
items.headline: Pose as a Friend, Work as a Spy
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You learned the real decision-maker wasn't the title 🎯
items.comment_q: Pitch the loudest exec or the silent gatekeeper first? 👇
items.body: The org chart said the director decided. The quiet chief of staff actually did. You briefed them first.

items.title: Law 15
items.headline: Crush Your Enemy Totally
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: If you take on a workplace rival, don't leave them wounded ⚔️
items.comment_q: Half-win a work conflict or end it cleanly? 👇
items.body: A half-beaten rival becomes a permanent enemy. Resolve it fully or don't start it.

items.title: Law 15
items.headline: Crush Your Enemy Totally
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Publicly embarrassing a peer creates a lifelong opponent ⚠️
items.comment_q: Crush a workplace rival or always leave them an exit? 👇
items.body: You 'won' the turf fight and humiliated them. They've been quietly working against you ever since.

items.title: Law 15
items.headline: Crush Your Enemy Totally
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You let a beaten rival save face. It came back as loyalty 🎯
items.comment_q: Give a defeated colleague a graceful out — soft or smart? 👇
items.body: You could have ended their credibility in that review. You didn't. They've defended you in rooms you're not in.

items.title: Law 16
items.headline: Use Absence to Increase Respect and Honor
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Strategic absence makes your contribution obvious 🌙
items.comment_q: Take real PTO and go dark — respected or risky? 👇
items.body: Be slightly less available and people feel your weight. Constant presence makes you furniture.

items.title: Law 16
items.headline: Use Absence to Increase Respect and Honor
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Being always-on trains them to expect you for free ⚠️
items.comment_q: Answer Slack on vacation: dedicated or doormat? 👇
items.body: You replied from the beach 'just this once,' every trip. Now off-hours you isn't a favor — it's the baseline.

items.title: Law 16
items.headline: Use Absence to Increase Respect and Honor
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You took a real week off. The gaps showed your value 🎯
items.comment_q: Fully unplug on leave or stay 'reachable for emergencies'? 👇
items.body: You went dark for seven days. Three things stalled without you. Your next review wrote itself.

items.title: Law 17
items.headline: Keep Others in Suspended Terror: Cultivate Unpredictability
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Be a little unpredictable — it keeps power off-balance 🎲
items.comment_q: Always-consistent or occasionally surprising — who's respected? 👇
items.body: Don't be the person whose every move is forecastable. A little unpredictability buys you leverage.

items.title: Law 17
items.headline: Keep Others in Suspended Terror: Cultivate Unpredictability
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Being 100% predictable makes you 100% easy to manage around ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is being totally reliable secretly making you replaceable? 👇
items.body: They could script your reaction to anything. So they planned around you instead of consulting you.

items.title: Law 17
items.headline: Keep Others in Suspended Terror: Cultivate Unpredictability
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You changed your usual answer once. The room recalibrated 🎯
items.comment_q: Push back when you always agree — power move or whiplash? 👇
items.body: You'd rubber-stamped every plan for a year. The one time you paused and questioned it, everyone leaned in.

items.title: Law 18
items.headline: Do Not Build Fortresses to Protect Yourself — Isolation Is Dangerous
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Don't wall yourself off — visibility is protection 🚪
items.comment_q: Heads-down isolation or stay in the mix to survive? 👇
items.body: The person nobody sees is the easiest to cut. Stay in the flow of information and people.

items.title: Law 18
items.headline: Do Not Build Fortresses to Protect Yourself — Isolation Is Dangerous
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Going heads-down 'to focus' is how you get forgotten ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is deep-work isolation quietly ending your career? 👇
items.body: You skipped the lunches and the syncs to grind. When opportunities got handed out, you weren't in the room.

items.title: Law 18
items.headline: Do Not Build Fortresses to Protect Yourself — Isolation Is Dangerous
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: The remote worker who never showed up got cut first 🎯
items.comment_q: Camera off and silent — efficient or endangered? 👇
items.body: Same performance, two people. The one who stayed visible in channels survived the reorg. The ghost didn't.

items.title: Law 19
items.headline: Know Who You're Dealing With — Do Not Offend the Wrong Person
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Know exactly who you're dealing with before you push 🧭
items.comment_q: Read someone's power before challenging them — always? 👇
items.body: Map the person's allies and ego before you cross them. The wrong target ends careers.

items.title: Law 19
items.headline: Know Who You're Dealing With — Do Not Offend the Wrong Person
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Snapping at the 'quiet' coworker who's wired to leadership ⚠️
items.comment_q: Underestimate a low-title colleague — survivable or fatal? 👇
items.body: You dismissed the junior PM in a meeting. Turns out she has the CEO's ear. That email aged badly.

items.title: Law 19
items.headline: Know Who You're Dealing With — Do Not Offend the Wrong Person
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You checked who someone knew before you opposed them 🎯
items.comment_q: Vet a rival's backers before the fight — paranoid or wise? 👇
items.body: Before pushing back on the proposal, you learned who sponsored it. You reframed instead of attacked. Smart.

items.title: Law 20
items.headline: Do Not Commit to Anyone
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Don't pick a side in a team feud — stay the neutral asset 🤝
items.comment_q: Pick a faction in office politics or stay independent? 👇
items.body: Commit to a camp and you inherit its enemies. Stay useful to all sides and you outlast the war.

items.title: Law 20
items.headline: Do Not Commit to Anyone
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Loudly joining one manager's camp burns the other bridge ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is being 'loyal to one boss' a trap when they leave? 👇
items.body: You were known as their person. They left. Their replacement cleaned house, starting with the loyalists.

items.title: Law 20
items.headline: Do Not Commit to Anyone
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Two directors at war. You stayed useful to both 🎯
items.comment_q: In a leadership turf war, ally or stay Switzerland? 👇
items.body: Everyone picked a side and got bloodied. You stayed neutral and competent. You're the one both still trust.

items.title: Law 21
items.headline: Play a Sucker to Catch a Sucker — Seem Dumber Than Your Mark
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Let them underestimate you — then deliver 🎭
items.comment_q: Play modest then overdeliver, or signal strength early? 👇
items.body: Sometimes the smartest move is to seem less sharp than you are until it counts.

items.title: Law 21
items.headline: Play a Sucker to Catch a Sucker — Seem Dumber Than Your Mark
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Showing all your cards in week one sets impossible expectations ⚠️
items.comment_q: Sprint hard on day one or pace the reveal? 👇
items.body: You stunned them in month one. Now that's the baseline, and 'merely excellent' reads as a decline.

items.title: Law 21
items.headline: Play a Sucker to Catch a Sucker — Seem Dumber Than Your Mark
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: They thought you'd miss it. You'd already shipped it 🎯
items.comment_q: Let a doubter underestimate you on purpose — smart? 👇
items.body: He assumed you couldn't handle the integration. You let him. You handed it done while he was still doubting.

items.title: Law 22
items.headline: Use the Surrender Tactic: Transform Weakness Into Power
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: When you can't win the fight, convert the surrender into time ⏳
items.comment_q: Concede a battle to win the war — weak or wise? 👇
items.body: Lose the unwinnable argument gracefully, keep your standing, and outlast the decision.

items.title: Law 22
items.headline: Use the Surrender Tactic: Transform Weakness Into Power
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Fighting a reorg you can't stop just marks you as the problem ⚠️
items.comment_q: Resist a doomed decision loudly or adapt fast? 👇
items.body: You waged war on the restructure for weeks. It happened anyway, and now you're 'not a team player.'

items.title: Law 22
items.headline: Use the Surrender Tactic: Transform Weakness Into Power
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You stopped resisting the new system and quietly mastered it 🎯
items.comment_q: Mandated tool you hate — protest or become the expert? 👇
items.body: Everyone complained about the migration. You learned it cold. Now you run the trainings and own the room.

items.title: Law 23
items.headline: Concentrate Your Forces
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Pour your energy into the one project that gets you seen 🎯
items.comment_q: Spread across five projects or own one that matters? 👇
items.body: Five half-owned projects make you forgettable. One visible, finished win makes your case.

items.title: Law 23
items.headline: Concentrate Your Forces
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Saying yes to everything dilutes the work that counts ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is being 'helpful on everything' wrecking your promotion case? 👇
items.body: You touched twenty things and owned none. At review time there was no single thing to point at.

items.title: Law 23
items.headline: Concentrate Your Forces
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You dropped the busywork and went all-in on one bet 🎯
items.comment_q: Cut your commitments to focus — brave or career risk? 👇
items.body: You renegotiated off three side projects. The one you kept became the line item in your promotion packet.

items.title: Law 24
items.headline: Play the Perfect Courtier
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Master the unwritten rules of how your org actually works 🎩
items.comment_q: Play the corporate game well or refuse on principle? 👇
items.body: Every company has a hidden etiquette. The ones who read it rise; the ones who scoff stall.

items.title: Law 24
items.headline: Play the Perfect Courtier
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Treating 'culture fit' rituals as beneath you costs you ⚠️
items.comment_q: Skip the office norms you find fake — integrity or error? 👇
items.body: You skipped the offsite, the lunches, the small talk. Skills intact, sponsorship gone.

items.title: Law 24
items.headline: Play the Perfect Courtier
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You learned how decisions really get made here 🎯
items.comment_q: Formal process or the real backchannel — which do you work? 👇
items.body: The approval 'process' was theater. Real calls happened in a Thursday huddle. You got yourself into it.

items.title: Law 25
items.headline: Re-Create Yourself
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Reintroduce yourself — upgrade the role people see you in 🦋
items.comment_q: Can you escape the 'junior' label at your own company? 👇
items.body: People freeze you at the version they met. Deliberately show a bigger one until it sticks.

items.title: Law 25
items.headline: Re-Create Yourself
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Letting your first-year reputation define year four ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is your old reputation at this company holding you hostage? 👇
items.body: You grew a lot. They still treat you like the nervous new hire because you never reset the image.

items.title: Law 25
items.headline: Re-Create Yourself
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You changed how you showed up. The room re-rated you 🎯
items.comment_q: Rebrand yourself internally or just job-hop to reset? 👇
items.body: New posture, sharper updates, fewer hedges. Within a quarter, leadership stopped talking past you.

items.title: Law 26
items.headline: Keep Your Hands Clean
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Stay out of the blast radius — keep your hands clean 🧼
items.comment_q: Distance from a doomed project early — loyal or smart? 👇
items.body: When something's going to fail, don't be the visible name on it. Proximity is blame.

items.title: Law 26
items.headline: Keep Your Hands Clean
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Defending a sinking project to look loyal sinks you with it ⚠️
items.comment_q: Go down with the failing project or step back early? 👇
items.body: You championed it to the end out of loyalty. It failed. Now you're the face of the failure.

items.title: Law 26
items.headline: Keep Your Hands Clean
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: The project imploded. You'd documented your warnings 🎯
items.comment_q: Paper-trail your concerns or trust verbal alignment? 👇
items.body: When it collapsed, the post-mortem hunted for blame. Your written, dated concerns kept your name clear.

items.title: Law 27
items.headline: Play on People's Need to Believe to Create a Cultlike Following
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Give people a mission to rally around and you lead it 🔥
items.comment_q: Inspire a team with a story or just assign the tasks? 👇
items.body: People follow meaning, not tickets. Frame the work as something worth believing and you're the leader.

items.title: Law 27
items.headline: Play on People's Need to Believe to Create a Cultlike Following
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Leading with process and no story loses the room ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does 'just hit the deadline' actually motivate anyone? 👇
items.body: You managed the plan perfectly and inspired no one. The team did the minimum and you wondered why.

items.title: Law 27
items.headline: Play on People's Need to Believe to Create a Cultlike Following
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You named the mission. The team started self-organizing 🎯
items.comment_q: Sell the why or just push the what — what moves people? 👇
items.body: You stopped listing tasks and named the outcome that mattered. People started bringing ideas unasked.

items.title: Law 28
items.headline: Enter Action With Boldness
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Pitch with total conviction — hesitation kills good ideas 💥
items.comment_q: Bold half-ready pitch or polished but timid — which wins? 👇
items.body: A confident ask beats a perfect one delivered apologetically. Commit in the room or don't go in.

items.title: Law 28
items.headline: Enter Action With Boldness
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Hedging your proposal invites everyone to poke holes ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does 'this might be dumb but…' ever land well? 👇
items.body: You opened with three disclaimers. They spent the meeting on your doubts instead of your idea.

items.title: Law 28
items.headline: Enter Action With Boldness
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You walked in certain. They approved it on the spot 🎯
items.comment_q: Ask for the budget boldly or build a careful case? 👇
items.body: Same numbers, different delivery. You stated it as the obvious move, not a request. They said yes.

items.title: Law 29
items.headline: Plan All the Way to the End
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Plan to the end — including who reacts and how 🧩
items.comment_q: Plan three moves ahead or adapt as it unfolds? 👇
items.body: Map the whole sequence before you start: approvals, pushback, who'll feel threatened. Surprises are unplanned failures.

items.title: Law 29
items.headline: Plan All the Way to the End
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Launching without thinking past step one detonates later ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is 'we'll figure it out' leadership or a time bomb? 👇
items.body: You shipped phase one with no plan for the politics of phase two. Phase two is where it died.

items.title: Law 29
items.headline: Plan All the Way to the End
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You'd already mapped the objections before the meeting 🎯
items.comment_q: Pre-game the objections or handle them live? 👇
items.body: Every pushback they raised, you'd pre-answered in the deck. The decision took ten minutes.

items.title: Law 30
items.headline: Make Your Accomplishments Seem Effortless
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Make the hard win look effortless — hide the grind 🪶
items.comment_q: Show the long hours or make it look easy — which earns more? 👇
items.body: Broadcasting how hard it was reads as struggling. Deliver clean and let it look natural.

items.title: Law 30
items.headline: Make Your Accomplishments Seem Effortless
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Performing your exhaustion makes you look barely capable ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does 'I was up till 2am on this' impress or worry leadership? 👇
items.body: You wanted credit for the effort. They heard 'this person is underwater' and scoped down your role.

items.title: Law 30
items.headline: Make Your Accomplishments Seem Effortless
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You delivered big and said it was 'no problem' 🎯
items.comment_q: Downplay the effort or claim every hour of it? 👇
items.body: It nearly broke you. You shipped it and shrugged. They started handing you the high-stakes work.

items.title: Law 31
items.headline: Control the Options: Get Others to Play With the Cards You Deal
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Frame the choice so every option still works for you ♟️
items.comment_q: Give your boss options or one recommendation? 👇
items.body: Don't ask open questions. Offer two paths you've already made sure you win either way.

items.title: Law 31
items.headline: Control the Options: Get Others to Play With the Cards You Deal
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Asking 'what should we do?' hands away your leverage ⚠️
items.comment_q: Open-ended questions to leadership: humble or weak? 👇
items.body: You asked the VP to decide from scratch. They picked the path that didn't need you.

items.title: Law 31
items.headline: Control the Options: Get Others to Play With the Cards You Deal
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You presented two options. Both routed through your team 🎯
items.comment_q: Control the menu or let leadership define it? 👇
items.body: You let them feel in charge by choosing — between two outcomes you'd already designed to favor you.

items.title: Law 32
items.headline: Play to People's Fantasies
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Sell the outcome people wish were true, then deliver toward it ✨
items.comment_q: Pitch the dream or the realistic plan — which gets funded? 👇
items.body: Dry plans don't get budget. Attach the work to the future they already want and watch doors open.

items.title: Law 32
items.headline: Play to People's Fantasies
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: All-spreadsheet, no-vision pitches die in committee ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does pure realism lose to a compelling story in funding? 👇
items.body: Your numbers were airtight and forgettable. The team with the vision and worse math got the headcount.

items.title: Law 32
items.headline: Play to People's Fantasies
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You painted the after-state. The room funded it 🎯
items.comment_q: Lead a pitch with data or with the vision? 👇
items.body: You opened with the world after the project shipped. By the time you reached the numbers, they were sold.

items.title: Law 33
items.headline: Discover Each Man's Thumbscrew
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Find what each stakeholder actually optimizes for 🔍
items.comment_q: Tailor your pitch per exec or one deck for all? 👇
items.body: Everyone has one thing they really protect: budget, risk, ego, time. Aim at it and the yes is fast.

items.title: Law 33
items.headline: Discover Each Man's Thumbscrew
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: One-size pitching to different stakeholders loses all of them ⚠️
items.comment_q: Same pitch to every exec — efficient or lazy? 👇
items.body: You ran the identical deck past finance and product. Neither felt it was for them. Both passed.

items.title: Law 33
items.headline: Discover Each Man's Thumbscrew
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You learned the CFO only cared about risk. You led with it 🎯
items.comment_q: Open with what they fear or what you want? 👇
items.body: She'd killed three proposals on risk. You opened with the risk plan. First one she approved all quarter.

items.title: Law 34
items.headline: Be Royal in Your Own Fashion: Act Like a King to Be Treated Like One
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Carry yourself like you're already at the next level 👑
items.comment_q: Act senior before the title or wait to be anointed? 👇
items.body: People treat you at the level you carry. Operate like the role you want before it's yours.

items.title: Law 34
items.headline: Be Royal in Your Own Fashion: Act Like a King to Be Treated Like One
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Acting junior 'until it's official' keeps it unofficial ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does waiting for the title to act senior backfire? 👇
items.body: You stayed in your lane waiting for permission. They promoted the person already behaving like the lead.

items.title: Law 34
items.headline: Be Royal in Your Own Fashion: Act Like a King to Be Treated Like One
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You started showing up like the manager. They made you one 🎯
items.comment_q: Step into authority early — presumptuous or proof? 👇
items.body: Before the title, you ran the meeting, owned the call, set the standard. The promotion just caught up.

items.title: Law 35
items.headline: Master the Art of Timing
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Time the ask — read when leadership can actually say yes ⏱️
items.comment_q: Pitch when it's ready or when they're receptive? 👇
items.body: The same request lands or dies on timing. Read the budget cycle and the mood before you ask.

items.title: Law 35
items.headline: Master the Art of Timing
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Great ask, terrible moment — denied on bad timing ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does timing beat merit when asking for a raise? 👇
items.body: You asked for the raise the week after the team missed target. Right case, fatally wrong week.

items.title: Law 35
items.headline: Master the Art of Timing
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You waited two weeks for the right moment. Instant yes 🎯
items.comment_q: Strike now or wait for the opening — what wins? 👇
items.body: You sat on the request until the big win landed. Asked Monday after. Approved before lunch.

items.title: Law 36
items.headline: Disdain Things You Cannot Have: Ignoring Them Is the Best Revenge
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Ignore the pointless slight — engaging makes it real 🙅
items.comment_q: Clap back at a passive-aggressive coworker or ignore it? 👇
items.body: Reacting to a jab elevates it. Disdain shrinks it. Pick the fights that move your career.

items.title: Law 36
items.headline: Disdain Things You Cannot Have: Ignoring Them Is the Best Revenge
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Litigating every small disrespect makes you the drama ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is responding to every slight strength or self-sabotage? 👇
items.body: You replied-all to set the record straight. Now you're 'difficult,' and they're still fine.

items.title: Law 36
items.headline: Disdain Things You Cannot Have: Ignoring Them Is the Best Revenge
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You let the snipe go. It died; they looked petty 🎯
items.comment_q: Address a public dig immediately or starve it? 👇
items.body: He took a shot in the meeting. You moved on like it didn't register. The room noted who looked small.

items.title: Law 37
items.headline: Create Compelling Spectacles
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Make your work a visible spectacle, not a buried doc 🎬
items.comment_q: A great demo or a great document — which gets remembered? 👇
items.body: A sharp visible moment beats a perfect file no one opens. Engineer the reveal.

items.title: Law 37
items.headline: Create Compelling Spectacles
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Burying your win in a doc nobody opens wastes it ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does a flawless report beat a memorable demo? 👇
items.body: Your analysis was brilliant and 40 pages deep. The team that made a 2-minute demo got the funding.

items.title: Law 37
items.headline: Create Compelling Spectacles
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You turned the result into a 90-second demo. It traveled 🎯
items.comment_q: Present the win live or send the deck? 👇
items.body: Instead of emailing the report, you ran a tight live demo. People forwarded it. Leadership asked for you.

items.title: Law 38
items.headline: Think as You Like but Behave Like Others
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Think independently — but don't broadcast every contrarian take 🎭
items.comment_q: Voice every disagreement or pick your moments? 👇
items.body: Keep your sharper opinions for where they pay. Constant contrarianism gets you labeled, not heard.

items.title: Law 38
items.headline: Think as You Like but Behave Like Others
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Being the meeting contrarian gets you uninvited ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is the 'devil's advocate' role respected or avoided? 👇
items.body: You challenged everything to seem rigorous. They stopped inviting you to the room where it's decided.

items.title: Law 38
items.headline: Think as You Like but Behave Like Others
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You held the contrarian take for the 1:1, not the room 🎯
items.comment_q: Disagree publicly or privately with leadership? 👇
items.body: You didn't torch the plan in the meeting. You raised it privately with the owner. It changed — quietly, with credit.

items.title: Law 39
items.headline: Stir Up Waters to Catch Fish
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Create motion to surface what people really think 🌊
items.comment_q: Stir a stalled decision or wait it out? 👇
items.body: A well-placed question can unstick a frozen team and reveal the real blockers and players.

items.title: Law 39
items.headline: Stir Up Waters to Catch Fish
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Stirring chaos you can't steer makes you the blocker ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is shaking things up leadership or just noise? 👇
items.body: You forced the conflict into the open with no plan to land it. Now you own the mess you exposed.

items.title: Law 39
items.headline: Stir Up Waters to Catch Fish
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You asked the question everyone avoided. It moved 🎯
items.comment_q: Name the elephant in the meeting or let it sit? 👇
items.body: Three meetings circled it. You said it plainly, calmly, once. The decision finally happened.

items.title: Law 40
items.headline: Despise the Free Lunch
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Be wary of the 'great opportunity' with no clear payoff 🎯
items.comment_q: Take the high-visibility project with no upside — yes/no? 👇
items.body: Free, urgent, 'great exposure' work often costs the most. Price every yes in time and credit.

items.title: Law 40
items.headline: Despise the Free Lunch
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Grabbing every 'opportunity' is how you burn out unseen ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is 'great for your growth' usually code for unpaid work? 👇
items.body: You took the stretch project 'for exposure.' It buried you, the credit went up, the raise didn't.

items.title: Law 40
items.headline: Despise the Free Lunch
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You said no to the glamorous trap. Dodged the bullet 🎯
items.comment_q: Decline a prestigious doomed project — brave or career risk? 👇
items.body: Everyone wanted the flashy initiative. You read the no-resources reality and passed. It cratered without you.

items.title: Law 41
items.headline: Avoid Stepping Into a Great Man's Shoes
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Following a legend? Don't imitate — define your own win 👞
items.comment_q: Take the rockstar's old role: copy them or reinvent it? 👇
items.body: Stepping into a star's seat by copying them guarantees the comparison kills you. Change the game.

items.title: Law 41
items.headline: Avoid Stepping Into a Great Man's Shoes
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Trying to be the beloved predecessor 2.0 sets you up to lose ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is replacing a popular leader a no-win unless you pivot? 👇
items.body: You ran it exactly like they did, hoping for the same love. You just looked like a worse copy.

items.title: Law 41
items.headline: Avoid Stepping Into a Great Man's Shoes
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You redefined the role instead of inheriting it 🎯
items.comment_q: Honor the predecessor's playbook or rewrite it? 👇
items.body: The legend's shoes were a trap. You scoped a different mandate. Now it's your win, not their echo.

items.title: Law 42
items.headline: Strike the Shepherd and the Sheep Will Scatter
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: In a hostile team, address the instigator, not the noise 🎯
items.comment_q: Manage the loud troublemaker or the whole team's mood? 👇
items.body: One person usually drives the dysfunction. Solve them and the rest of the team resets.

items.title: Law 42
items.headline: Strike the Shepherd and the Sheep Will Scatter
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Disciplining the whole team for one person's behavior ⚠️
items.comment_q: Punish the group for one bad actor — fair or fatal? 👇
items.body: You added rules for everyone to avoid confronting one person. The good people felt punished and left.

items.title: Law 42
items.headline: Strike the Shepherd and the Sheep Will Scatter
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You handled the source. The team's tension dissolved 🎯
items.comment_q: Confront the ringleader directly or work around them? 👇
items.body: One person seeded every standup with cynicism. You addressed them one-on-one. The mood flipped in a week.

items.title: Law 43
items.headline: Work on the Hearts and Minds of Others
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Win cooperation through what they care about, not authority ❤️
items.comment_q: Lead through buy-in or through positional power? 👇
items.body: Orders get compliance; understanding their stakes gets commitment. Work the heart and the mind.

items.title: Law 43
items.headline: Work on the Hearts and Minds of Others
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Pulling rank to force a 'yes' breeds quiet sabotage ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does 'because I said so' management ever actually work? 👇
items.body: You mandated it. They complied to your face and slow-walked it everywhere you couldn't see.

items.title: Law 43
items.headline: Work on the Hearts and Minds of Others
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You sold the why first. They executed without being pushed 🎯
items.comment_q: Mandate the change or build the case for it? 👇
items.body: Instead of the directive, you showed each person what it solved for them. Adoption happened on its own.

items.title: Law 44
items.headline: Disarm and Infuriate With the Mirror Effect
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Mirror a difficult colleague's style to defuse them 🪞
items.comment_q: Match a hostile coworker's energy or stay neutral? 👇
items.body: Reflecting someone's own approach back disarms them and reveals their game without you saying a word.

items.title: Law 44
items.headline: Disarm and Infuriate With the Mirror Effect
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Out-aggressing an aggressive coworker escalates into your problem ⚠️
items.comment_q: Fight fire with fire at work — effective or career-ending? 👇
items.body: You matched their hostility blow for blow. Now there are two problems, and one of them is you.

items.title: Law 44
items.headline: Disarm and Infuriate With the Mirror Effect
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You calmly mirrored their tactic. They backed down 🎯
items.comment_q: Expose a manipulator by mirroring or by confronting? 👇
items.body: Every time they deflected, you reflected it back evenly. The pattern became obvious to the whole room — not from you.

items.title: Law 45
items.headline: Preach the Need for Change, but Never Reform Too Much at Once
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Push change in small doses people can absorb 🔧
items.comment_q: Big-bang reorg or incremental change — which actually sticks? 👇
items.body: Announce the need for change, then move in increments. Too much at once triggers the immune response.

items.title: Law 45
items.headline: Preach the Need for Change, but Never Reform Too Much at Once
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Ripping up everything on day one gets the new lead rejected ⚠️
items.comment_q: Should a new manager change things fast or earn it first? 👇
items.body: You overhauled the whole process in week two. The team didn't resist the change — they resisted you.

items.title: Law 45
items.headline: Preach the Need for Change, but Never Reform Too Much at Once
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You changed one thing, proved it, then the next 🎯
items.comment_q: Earn trust before reform or reform to prove yourself? 👇
items.body: You shipped one small visible improvement first. The win bought you permission for the big one.

items.title: Law 46
items.headline: Never Appear Too Perfect
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Stay relatable — flawless coworkers attract quiet resentment 🫧
items.comment_q: Is being the 'perfect' employee making you a target? 👇
items.body: A small visible humility or shared struggle buys allies. Untouchable people get undermined.

items.title: Law 46
items.headline: Never Appear Too Perfect
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Performing perfection isolates you from your own team ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does the 'never wrong' colleague get respect or knives? 👇
items.body: You never admitted a miss. The team stopped rooting for you and started waiting for the fall.

items.title: Law 46
items.headline: Never Appear Too Perfect
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You owned a small mistake openly. Trust went up 🎯
items.comment_q: Admit a mistake to your team or protect the image? 👇
items.body: You said 'that call was mine and it was wrong' in standup. The room relaxed. People started leveling with you.

items.title: Law 47
items.headline: Do Not Go Past the Mark You Aimed For; In Victory, Learn When to Stop
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Hit the goal and stop — overreach undoes the win 🛑
items.comment_q: Quit while ahead or push for the bigger swing? 👇
items.body: The win is fragile right after you get it. Consolidate before you chase more.

items.title: Law 47
items.headline: Do Not Go Past the Mark You Aimed For; In Victory, Learn When to Stop
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Pushing for more right after a win often gives it all back ⚠️
items.comment_q: After a big win, press the advantage or lock it in? 👇
items.body: You parlayed the promotion into an immediate bigger ask. The greed read poorly and cost you the goodwill.

items.title: Law 47
items.headline: Do Not Go Past the Mark You Aimed For; In Victory, Learn When to Stop
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You banked the win instead of pressing it. Smart 🎯
items.comment_q: Capitalize on momentum or secure the gain first? 👇
items.body: You'd just landed the lead role. You resisted the urge to renegotiate everything. You looked solid, not greedy.

items.title: Law 48
items.headline: Assume Formlessness
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Stay adaptable — rigid plans break in real orgs 🌊
items.comment_q: Hold the plan or bend with the org — which survives? 👇
items.body: Orgs shift under you. The people who flex with the reorg outlast the ones married to last quarter's plan.

items.title: Law 48
items.headline: Assume Formlessness
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: Rigidly defending 'the original plan' marks you as stuck ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is sticking to the plan discipline or denial? 👇
items.body: The market moved, the priorities moved, and you defended the Q1 roadmap into Q3. You moved with it — out.

items.title: Law 48
items.headline: Assume Formlessness
items.author: Robert Greene
items.tease: You dropped the dead plan fast. You looked like a leader 🎯
items.comment_q: Abandon a failing strategy early or see it through? 👇
items.body: When the data turned, you killed your own initiative first and pivoted. Leadership noticed who wasn't precious.

items.title: Atomic Habits
items.headline: Tiny Changes, Remarkable Results
items.author: James Clear
items.series_label: ATOMIC HABITS  ·  1/30  ·  SUBSCRIBE FOR THE REST
items.tease: Your career is built by tiny daily reps, not big swings 🧱
items.comment_q: Career growth: one big move or 100 small ones? 👇
items.body: The promotion is a lagging indicator of a year of small, boring, repeated work habits.

items.title: Atomic Habits
items.headline: Tiny Changes, Remarkable Results
items.author: James Clear
items.series_label: ATOMIC HABITS  ·  1/30  ·  SUBSCRIBE FOR THE REST
items.tease: Waiting for the 'big break' is why you're still stuck ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is 'waiting for the right opportunity' a plan or a cope? 👇
items.body: Nobody gets discovered. They get 1% better daily until they're undeniable.

items.title: Atomic Habits
items.headline: Tiny Changes, Remarkable Results
items.author: James Clear
items.series_label: ATOMIC HABITS  ·  1/30  ·  SUBSCRIBE FOR THE REST
items.tease: Two hires, year one. One did the boring reps 🎯
items.comment_q: Flashy output or quiet consistency — who lasts? 👇
items.body: Same start. One chased visible wins, one compounded small ones. Year two wasn't close.

items.title: Compound Effect
items.headline: 1% Better Every Day
items.author: James Clear
items.series_label: ATOMIC HABITS  ·  2/30  ·  SUBSCRIBE FOR THE REST
items.tease: One sharper email a day compounds into authority 📈
items.comment_q: Does small daily improvement actually beat raw talent? 👇
items.body: Get 1% better at one work skill daily. In a year you're the person they consult.

items.title: Compound Effect
items.headline: 1% Better Every Day
items.author: James Clear
items.series_label: ATOMIC HABITS  ·  2/30  ·  SUBSCRIBE FOR THE REST
items.tease: 'It's just one skipped week' is how careers flatline ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is an off-week harmless or the start of the slide? 👇
items.body: The decline isn't a cliff. It's small skipped reps no one notices until your role shrinks.

items.title: Compound Effect
items.headline: 1% Better Every Day
items.author: James Clear
items.series_label: ATOMIC HABITS  ·  2/30  ·  SUBSCRIBE FOR THE REST
items.tease: She spent 20 min/day learning the tool. Year later she owns it 🎯
items.comment_q: 20 minutes daily upskilling or weekend cram sessions? 👇

items.body: You felt like you were improving. No record, no proof, no momentum. The feeling faded; nothing held it up.

items.title: Tracking

items.title: Never Miss Twice

items.body: The rule isn't 'never miss.' It's 'never miss two in a row.' That single rule saves every habit.

items.title: Never Miss Twice

items.body: One miss became 'the week's blown,' became a month off. The miss didn't end it. The story did.

items.title: Never Miss Twice

items.comment_q: After a slip: restart now or 'next Monday'? 👇
items.body: Skipped a day, felt the pull to quit, showed up the next day anyway. That one choice kept the streak's spirit alive.

items.body: Tell your manager the thing you'll ship by Friday. Public stakes finish what private intentions don't.

items.title: Accountability

items.body: No one knew, so no one noticed when you stopped — including, eventually, you.

items.title: Accountability

items.body: She announced 'demo Friday' in standup. The public date did what three private to-do lists hadn't.

items.comment_q: Stretch slightly or stay where you're comfortable? 👇
items.body: Growth lives at the edge of your ability — hard enough to engage, not so hard you stall.

items.title: Goldilocks

items.body: You did what you were already good at for two years. You didn't fail. You just didn't grow, and it showed.

items.title: Goldilocks

items.body: He took the project he wasn't quite ready for. Sweated it, shipped it, and came out a level higher.

items.title: Reflection

items.title: Reflection
items.headline: Review and Refine

items.series_label: ATOMIC HABITS  ·  27/30  ·  SUBSCRIBE FOR THE REST
items.tease: She did Friday reviews. Her mistakes stopped repeating 🎯
items.comment_q: Weekly reflection ritual or just keep moving? 👇

items.body: Even good habits decay when you stop paying attention. Periodically check you're still aimed right.

items.title: Mindfulness

items.body: You ran the same play for years without checking if it still worked. It stopped working a while ago.

items.title: Mindfulness

items.body: A quarterly honest check flagged the drift. He adjusted before it became someone else's note about him.

items.title: 12 Rules for Life
items.headline: An Antidote to Chaos
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Order beats chaos — run your work life on rules 🧭
items.comment_q: Do personal operating rules help at work or overthink it? 👇
items.body: A few hard personal rules — when you speak, what you commit to — make the chaotic office navigable.

items.title: 12 Rules for Life
items.headline: An Antidote to Chaos
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Winging office politics with no principles is why it eats you ⚠️
items.comment_q: Improvise every workplace situation or have set rules? 👇
items.body: With no rules, every hard moment was decided by your worst impulse in the moment. It showed.

items.title: 12 Rules for Life
items.headline: An Antidote to Chaos
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: She had three non-negotiable work rules. They held 🎯
items.comment_q: Are personal work rules a crutch or an edge? 👇
items.body: Three rules she never broke under pressure. In the messy quarters, they were the reason she stayed trusted.

items.title: Rule 1
items.headline: Stand Up Straight With Your Shoulders Back
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Posture and presence change how seriously you're taken 🦅
items.comment_q: Does physical presence really affect workplace authority? 👇
items.body: Shoulders back, steady voice, take the space. People assign competence to presence before they hear your point.

items.title: Rule 1
items.headline: Stand Up Straight With Your Shoulders Back
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Shrinking in the room signals 'don't take me seriously' ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is the quiet hunched contributor overlooked unfairly or predictably? 👇
items.body: Your work was strong and your body said 'sorry to exist.' The room read the body first.

items.title: Rule 1
items.headline: Stand Up Straight With Your Shoulders Back
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: He changed how he entered meetings. He got interrupted less 🎯
items.comment_q: Does standing tall in meetings change outcomes or not? 👇
items.body: Same arguments, new presence — slower, grounded, unhurried. People stopped talking over him within a week.

items.title: Rule 2
items.headline: Treat Yourself Like Someone You Are Responsible for Helping
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Manage your career like you'd manage someone you're responsible for 💼
items.comment_q: Would you let a report self-neglect like you do? 👇
items.body: You'd never let a junior skip growth, rest, or advocacy. Give yourself the same management.

items.title: Rule 2
items.headline: Treat Yourself Like Someone You Are Responsible for Helping
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Neglecting your own development while serving everyone else's ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is being endlessly 'helpful' generous or self-erasing? 👇
items.body: You invested in everyone's projects and none of your own. Helpful, invisible, and overdue for a hard look.

items.title: Rule 2
items.headline: Treat Yourself Like Someone You Are Responsible for Helping
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: She booked her own growth like a report's 1:1 🎯
items.comment_q: Schedule your own development or hope it happens? 👇
items.body: She gave herself the standing dev-time she'd give any report. In a year she was the one giving the 1:1s.

items.title: Rule 3
items.headline: Make Friends With People Who Want the Best for You
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Build your circle from people who want you to win 🤝
items.comment_q: Do your closest work allies actually root for you? 👇
items.body: Audit who you spend office energy on. Drift toward the ones who push you up, not the ones who anchor you.

items.title: Rule 3
items.headline: Make Friends With People Who Want the Best for You
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Loyalty to people who keep you small is a slow career tax ⚠️
items.comment_q: Stay loyal to a draining work clique or move on? 👇
items.body: You stayed close to the group that mocked ambition. You absorbed their ceiling and called it loyalty.

items.title: Rule 3
items.headline: Make Friends With People Who Want the Best for You
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: He changed who he ate lunch with. His trajectory changed 🎯
items.comment_q: Do your lunch people lift you or level you down? 👇
items.body: New table: people building things. Old table: people complaining. Six months later only one group had moved.

items.title: Rule 4
items.headline: Compare Yourself to Who You Were Yesterday
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Benchmark against yesterday-you, not the office star 📈
items.comment_q: Compare to top performers or to your past self? 👇
items.body: Measuring against the team's best burns you out. Measuring against last-quarter-you compounds you forward.

items.title: Rule 4
items.headline: Compare Yourself to Who You Were Yesterday
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Comparing to the top performer is a motivation killer ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does comparing up drive you or quietly demoralize you? 👇
items.body: You measured every week against the team's star and felt like nothing. The metric, not the work, was the problem.

items.title: Rule 4
items.headline: Compare Yourself to Who You Were Yesterday
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: She tracked her own delta. Confidence and output rose 🎯
items.comment_q: Is self-comparison healthier fuel than peer-comparison? 👇
items.body: She stopped watching the top performer and started beating last month's self. Both her mood and her reviews climbed.

items.title: Rule 5
items.headline: Do Not Let Children Do Things That Make You Dislike Them
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Don't tolerate behavior that erodes your respect for a report 🧱
items.comment_q: Address a likable report's bad habit early or let it slide? 👇
items.body: Small unaddressed behaviors curdle into resentment you can't hide. Correct it early, kindly, before you dislike them.

items.title: Rule 5
items.headline: Do Not Let Children Do Things That Make You Dislike Them
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Avoiding the hard feedback is how good people quietly fail ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is withholding tough feedback kindness or cowardice? 👇
items.body: You spared them the hard conversation for a year. The PIP was the first time they ever heard it. That's on you.

items.title: Rule 5
items.headline: Do Not Let Children Do Things That Make You Dislike Them
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: He gave the uncomfortable feedback at week two, not month ten 🎯
items.comment_q: Early blunt feedback or wait for the 'right moment'? 👇
items.body: Awkward two-minute conversation early. It saved the report, the project, and a relationship a late PIP would've ended.

items.title: Rule 6
items.headline: Set Your House in Order Before You Criticize the World
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Fix your own work before you criticize the org 🧹
items.comment_q: Earn the right to critique by delivering first — fair? 👇
items.body: Criticism from someone whose own house is in order lands. From someone whose isn't, it's just noise.

items.title: Rule 6
items.headline: Set Your House in Order Before You Criticize the World
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Loudly diagnosing the company while your own work slips ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is the office critic insightful or just deflecting? 👇
items.body: You had strong opinions on everything broken and a backlog of your own. People noticed which came first.

items.title: Rule 6
items.headline: Set Your House in Order Before You Criticize the World
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: She nailed her delivery, then her critique got heard 🎯
items.comment_q: Critique up only after your own results are clean? 👇
items.body: Once her own work was undeniable, the same feedback she'd given for months suddenly carried weight.

items.title: Rule 7
items.headline: Pursue What Is Meaningful, Not What Is Expedient
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Choose the meaningful project over the easy visible one 🎯
items.comment_q: Take the quick-win optics or the hard meaningful work? 👇
items.body: Expedient wins fade by next quarter. The hard problem you actually solved becomes your reputation.

items.title: Rule 7
items.headline: Pursue What Is Meaningful, Not What Is Expedient
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Chasing only quick wins builds a thin, brittle reputation ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is a string of easy wins a career or a house of cards? 👇
items.body: You optimized for fast optics for two years. When the hard problem came, no one trusted you with it.

items.title: Rule 7
items.headline: Pursue What Is Meaningful, Not What Is Expedient
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: He took the unglamorous hard problem. It defined him 🎯
items.comment_q: Be known for many quick wins or one hard solve? 👇
items.body: Everyone chased the visible quick wins. He took the ugly foundational problem. Now he's the person they call.

items.title: Rule 8
items.headline: Tell the Truth — or, at Least, Don't Lie
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Don't lie at work — especially the small convenient ones 🎯
items.comment_q: Is a small status-update white lie harmless or corrosive? 👇
items.body: The 'almost done' that wasn't, the 'no issues' that had them. Small lies compound into a trust deficit you can't repay.

items.title: Rule 8
items.headline: Tell the Truth — or, at Least, Don't Lie
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Optimistic status lies are the fastest way to lose trust ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does 'on track' when it isn't ever end well? 👇
items.body: You said green to avoid the hard conversation. The red surfaced anyway, plus now you're the one who hid it.

items.title: Rule 8
items.headline: Tell the Truth — or, at Least, Don't Lie
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: She flagged the slip early and honestly. Trust went up 🎯
items.comment_q: Report a slip the day it happens or hope to recover? 👇
items.body: She said 'this is at risk and here's why' on day one. Uncomfortable, and the reason leadership now believes her greens.

items.title: Rule 9
items.headline: Assume the Person You Are Listening To Knows Something You Don't
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Assume the person you're dismissing knows something you don't 👂
items.comment_q: Listen to learn or wait for your turn to talk? 👇
items.body: Walk into the disagreement assuming they see something you can't yet. You'll catch the thing that saves the project.

items.title: Rule 9
items.headline: Assume the Person You Are Listening To Knows Something You Don't
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Dismissing 'obviously wrong' colleagues is how you miss the catch ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is the person you tune out usually wrong — or inconvenient? 👇
items.body: You'd already decided they were wrong, so you didn't hear the risk they were flagging. It shipped. It broke.

items.title: Rule 9
items.headline: Assume the Person You Are Listening To Knows Something You Don't
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: He actually listened to the junior's objection. It saved the launch 🎯
items.comment_q: Hear out the junior's pushback or trust your read? 👇
items.body: The newest person raised a quiet concern. He almost talked past it. He didn't, and it was the bug that mattered.

items.title: Rule 10
items.headline: Be Precise in Your Speech
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Be precise — vague asks at work create vague disasters 🎯
items.comment_q: Is most workplace conflict really just imprecise language? 👇
items.body: 'Soon,' 'mostly done,' 'handle it.' Name the exact deliverable, owner, and date or own the mess that follows.

items.title: Rule 10
items.headline: Be Precise in Your Speech
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Vague commitments are the root of most missed deadlines ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is 'I'll get it to you soon' a commitment or an escape? 👇
items.body: Nobody lied. 'Soon' just meant four different things to four people, and the deadline absorbed all four.

items.title: Rule 10
items.headline: Be Precise in Your Speech
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: She forced precise asks. The team stopped missing deadlines 🎯
items.comment_q: Pin exact dates/owners or keep it flexible and friendly? 👇
items.body: Every action item got a name and a date or it didn't leave the meeting. Slips dropped to near zero in a month.

items.title: Rule 11
items.headline: Do Not Bother Children When They Are Skateboarding
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Let competent people take risks — don't smother the bold ones 🛹
items.comment_q: Should you shield a high performer from every risk? 👇
items.body: Over-protecting your best people from stretch and failure is how you make them mediocre and resentful.

items.title: Rule 11
items.headline: Do Not Bother Children When They Are Skateboarding
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Micromanaging your strongest performer is how you lose them ⚠️
items.comment_q: Does heavy oversight protect quality or kill your best people? 👇
items.body: You hovered to keep standards high. Your strongest hire felt distrusted, stopped taking initiative, then left.

items.title: Rule 11
items.headline: Do Not Bother Children When They Are Skateboarding
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: He stopped hovering. His best engineer leveled up fast 🎯
items.comment_q: Give a top performer rope or keep a tight leash? 👇
items.body: He backed off the daily check-ins. Given room to risk and recover, she shipped the thing he couldn't have.

items.title: Rule 12
items.headline: Pet a Cat When You Encounter One on the Street
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: In a brutal quarter, take the small recovery wins 🐈
items.comment_q: Is acknowledging small wins soft or survival? 👇
items.body: When everything's on fire, the people who notice and bank the tiny good moments are the ones still standing at Q4.

items.title: Rule 12
items.headline: Pet a Cat When You Encounter One on the Street
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: Grinding through chaos with zero recovery is how you burn out ⚠️
items.comment_q: Is 'push through, no breaks' resilience or a countdown? 👇
items.body: You powered through the death-march quarter refusing every small pause. You hit the deadline and then hit a wall.

items.title: Rule 12
items.headline: Pet a Cat When You Encounter One on the Street
items.author: Jordan Peterson
items.tease: She protected small recovery moments mid-crisis. She lasted 🎯
items.comment_q: In a crunch, do micro-breaks help or just slow you? 👇
items.body: Everyone sprinted to empty. She took the small daily resets. At the end, she was the one still able to think.
